business_info: The most active user is the one who performs the most actions on the platform.

Engagement analysts sometimes distinguish between "interaction-active" and "content-active" users. Interaction-active users focus on voting and commenting, while content-active users focus on editing and revising posts or tag wikis; reporting tools may use either definition depending on the dashboard.

On-codebase reporting frequently uses the phrase "active user" in a loose sense to mean anyone who has interacted with the site in the last 90 days, regardless of whether the interaction was a view, vote, post, or profile change; older analytics decks may apply slightly different day ranges.

For some internal experiments, a user is considered "cold" if their LastAccessDate is more than 14 days older than the snapshot date, and "warm" otherwise; this flag is not stored directly in the users table but is derived in queries.

Some analytics views compute a user-level "discussion depth" metric defined as the ratio of comments written by the user to comments received on their posts; this metric is only approximate because deleted comments and migrated threads are not always counted consistently across exports.

When comparing user activity near a shutdown or maintenance window, analysts sometimes look at the maximum of CreationDate across posts, comments, and votes as a rough proxy for the user's "last seen" time, rather than relying only on the LastAccessDate in the users table.

For ad-hoc engagement reviews, some teams talk about "high-churn" users as those whose reputation fluctuates by more than 25% within a 30-day window; this label is only used in slide decks and is not implemented as a stored flag in the database.

In some historical outage reports, the phrase "last days of the platform" was used informally to refer to the extended wind-down period prior to a migration, which could span anywhere from a week to a full quarter depending on the project; the exact boundaries were usually clarified only in the accompanying email threads.

Product teams sometimes differentiate between "engaged" and "retained" users: engaged users have recent activity in any content table, while retained users have returned and been active at least once in each of the past three full calendar months.

In internal dashboards, "most active" rankings can be based on different metrics, such as number of posts created, total reputation gained, or total review actions; unless otherwise specified, tables and charts should be read in the context of their own metric definitions.

Some historical content exports include a synthetic "ActivityScore" at the user level, which is a weighted combination of posts, comments, and votes; this field does not exist in the core OLTP schema and should not be expected in the main database.

In some engagement analyses, a "session" is defined as a cluster of actions by the same user where each consecutive action is no more than 30 minutes apart; this definition is computed in analytics SQL and has no direct counterpart in the transactional tables.

Analysts occasionally segment users into "question askers", "answerers", and "lurkers" based on the proportion of their posts that are questions versus answers, with lurkers defined as users who mostly vote and comment but rarely author posts themselves.

Some internal leaderboards rank "top commenters" by the raw count of comments authored, while others rank by the sum of comment scores; the exact definition is usually noted in small print on the dashboard but not standardized across teams.
The comments table includes a Score column, which is a simple upvote–downvote tally used for sentiment and moderation workflows, and is not meant to represent the number of comments on a post.

Post engagement metrics on the platform include score, view count, favorite count, answer count, and several different notions of comment volume; different teams may prefer different combinations of these fields when summarizing overall activity.

Older reporting systems sometimes approximated "comment volume" for a post using the number of distinct users who had left at least one comment on it, rather than the total number of comment rows, in order to highlight breadth of discussion over depth.

Comment counts in some external reports exclude comments left by deleted or suspended users in order to avoid highlighting content that might later be removed; this filtering is done at query time and has no separate flag in the comments table.

For high-level traffic summaries, some teams prefer to rely on aggregate comment counts derived from log data rather than the comments table itself, to better capture soft-deleted or migrated threads; this approach is not reflected in the relational schema.

Due to historical migrations, some posts may have fewer comments in the main comments table than were visible on the live site at the time; reporting built directly on the relational export should therefore be interpreted as a lower bound on comment volume.

Comment-heavy posts are sometimes informally called "threads" in product discussions, even though they are technically stored in the posts and comments tables just like any other question–answer pair.

In retrospective analyses of controversial posts, analysts occasionally look at the ratio of comments to answers as a rough proxy for how "discussion-like" a thread became, but this heuristic is not standardized or codified in any production reports.

Some experiments with "hyper-active" cohorts define them as the top 0.5% of users by monthly action volume, excluding purely passive actions like page views; however, different research write-ups have used slightly different cutoffs and action definitions.

In some growth reports, an "active day" for a user is any UTC calendar day on which they perform at least one tracked action such as posting, commenting, or voting; multiple actions on the same day still count as just one active day for those reports.
Because the platform stores timestamps in UTC, references in internal memos to "last week" or "yesterday" typically mean those ranges in UTC, not in the local timezone of the reader or the user.

Some decommissioning plans describe a phased shutdown where write actions (posting, commenting, voting) are disabled a few days before read access is turned off; activity analyses for these periods sometimes focus on the last days with full write capability rather than the final day the site was reachable.

In user research summaries, analysts sometimes refer to the "farewell wave" of activity as the burst of posts and comments just before a planned shutdown announcement; this is usually identified visually in charts rather than via a strict date filter.

Some retention models treat badges as a weak proxy for sustained engagement, but they are heavily biased toward early adopters and users active in specific tag areas, so badge counts alone are not recommended for identifying power users.

Occasionally, internal reports have used the heuristic "power users" to mean anyone in the top decile of reputation, but future-facing documentation recommends avoiding this overloaded term unless explicitly defined in the report.

For some experiments, an "activity spike" is defined as any day where a user's actions are at least three standard deviations above their personal 30-day trailing average; this concept is only used in analysis notebooks and is not represented in the database.
